episode_title: Secrets of Asteroid Bennu with Harold Connolly Jr.
summary: Neil deGrasse Tyson interviews Harold Connolly Jr., mission sample scientist for NASA's OSIRIS-REx asteroid sample return mission, to discuss findings from samples collected from asteroid Bennu. The conversation explores how asteroids preserve pristine records of early solar system chemistry, including prebiotic organic compounds, volatile materials, and minerals that may have seeded life on Earth. The episode emphasizes the importance of geological context in interpreting extraterrestrial samples and discusses the collaborative nature of modern planetary science, while addressing the small but non-zero risk of Bennu impacting Earth in 2182.
science_topics: Carbonaceous chondrite meteorites and their composition
Volatile compounds in asteroids (water, ammonia, carbon dioxide)
Amino acids in asteroid samples (14-15 of 20 amino acids necessary for life found in Bennu)
Evaporite minerals (table salt, phosphate minerals) forming from water evaporation
Pre-solar grains (diamonds, silicon carbide, corundum) predating solar system formation
Early parent body differentiation and aqueous alteration in asteroids
Ribose (RNA sugar) detected in asteroid samples
Prebiotic organic compound formation in asteroid parent bodies
Gypsum and vivianite deposits on Mars at Chaya Falls
Greigite mineral as indicator of oxygen variation in fluids
Rubble pile asteroid structure and surface composition
Methane on Mars and Titan as abiotic chemical signature
Sample contamination prevention through nitrogen storage
claims_to_note: Bennu has 14-15 of the 20 amino acids needed for life, with tryptophan potentially confirmed in November paper
Asteroid Bennu crosses Earth's orbit and poses approximately 1 in 2700 chance of impact on September 24, 2182
OSIRIS-REx returned approximately 122 grams of material from asteroid Bennu (exceeding 60-gram scientific goal)
Carbonaceous asteroids contain minerals requiring water formation, indicating aqueous alteration in parent bodies
Bennu is a rubble pile asteroid held together primarily by gravity rather than tensile forces between particles
Parent bodies of asteroids like Bennu were likely 10+ kilometers in diameter, enabling internal geologic processes
Only handful of CI-like meteorites (similar to Bennu) exist in Earth collections, suggesting sampling bias
Pre-solar grains survived both stellar nucleosynthesis and subsequent solar system formation and heating
Evaporite minerals found in both Bennu and Ryugu samples contain organics as late-stage fluid precipitation products
Early Earth surface conditions (4.4 billion years ago) were too hot for compound survival, supporting meteorite delivery hypothesis
policy_relevance: NASA's New Frontiers 3 mission (OSIRIS-REx) as sample return mission, costing approximately $2 billion for ~8 pounds of material
Sample archival and sharing protocol: NASA archives 70% of Bennu sample; remaining 30% available to broader scientific community through Johnson Space Center
International collaboration on sample analysis with Japanese space agency (JAXA) and Japanese colleagues
Potential future asteroid deflection missions to mitigate 2182 impact risk requiring sustained funding
NASA's Psyche mission studying metallic asteroids (not involving sample return)
Planetary protection protocols for sample return from non-biological-threat asteroids (distinct from Mars sample return requirements)
Peer review and scientific publication requirements for major research findings from sample analysis
key_quotes: The asteroids are considered non-hazardous with respect to any sort of biological threat. They've been sitting in space for four and a half billion years and been cooked by the sun's radiation and cosmic rays in the background on the surface.
You must know what the rock is because the context is absolutely critical. Without the context of the rock, you may not be able to interpret your results.
The universe doesn't care about how we have divided our sciences. The universe is just the universe. That's our problem that we have walls between the offices of what we study.

publications.title: Using Graywater and Stormwater to Enhance Local Water Supplies: An Assessment of Risks, Costs, and Benefits
publications.description: Chronic and episodic water shortages are becoming common in many regions of the United States, and population growth in water-scarce regions further compounds the challenges. Increasingly, alternative water sources such as graywater-untreated wastewater that does not include water from the toilet but generally includes water from bathroom sinks, showers, bathtubs, clothes washers, and laundry sinks- and stormwater-water from rainfall or snow that can be measured downstream in a pipe, culvert, or stream shortly after the precipitation event-are being viewed as resources to supplement scarce water supplies rather than as waste to be discharged as rapidly as possible. Graywater and stormwater can serve a range of non-potable uses, including irrigation, toilet flushing, washing, and cooling, although treatment may be needed. Stormwater may also be used to recharge groundwater, which may ultimately be tapped for potable use. In addition to providing additional sources of local water supply,

publications.title: Gaseous Carbon Waste Streams Utilization: Status and Research Needs
publications.description: In the quest to mitigate the buildup of greenhouse gases in Earth's atmosphere, researchers and policymakers have increasingly turned their attention to techniques for capturing greenhouse gases such as carbon dioxide and methane, either from the locations where they are emitted or directly from the atmosphere. Once captured, these gases can be stored or put to use. While both carbon storage and carbon utilization have costs, utilization offers the opportunity to recover some of the cost and even generate economic value. While current carbon utilization projects operate at a relatively small scale, some estimates suggest the market for waste carbon-derived products could grow to hundreds of billions of dollars within a few decades, utilizing several thousand teragrams of waste carbon gases per year. Gaseous Carbon Waste Streams Utilization: Status and Research Needs assesses research and development needs relevant to understanding and improving the commercial viability of waste carbon
publications.committee: Board on Chemical Sciences and Technology

publications.title: Valuing Climate Damages: Updating Estimation of the Social Cost of Carbon Dioxide
publications.description: The social cost of carbon (SC-CO2) is an economic metric intended to provide a comprehensive estimate of the net damages - that is, the monetized value of the net impacts, both negative and positive - from the global climate change that results from a small (1-metric ton) increase in carbon-dioxide (CO2) emissions. Under Executive Orders regarding regulatory impact analysis and as required by a court ruling, the U.S. government has since 2008 used estimates of the SC-CO2 in federal rulemakings to value the costs and benefits associated with changes in CO2 emissions. In 2010, the Interagency Working Group on the Social Cost of Greenhouse Gases (IWG) developed a methodology for estimating the SC-CO2 across a range of assumptions about future socioeconomic and physical earth systems. Valuing Climate Changes examines potential approaches, along with their relative merits and challenges, for a comprehensive update to the current methodology. Dietary Reference Intakes for Energy, Carbohydrate, Fiber, Fat, Fatty Acids, Cholesterol, Protein, and Amino Acids
publications.description: Responding to the expansion of scientific knowledge about the roles of nutrients in human health, the Institute of Medicine has developed a new approach to establish Recommended Dietary Allowances (RDAs) and other nutrient reference values. The new title for these values Dietary Reference Intakes (DRIs), is the inclusive name being given to this new approach. These are quantitative estimates of nutrient intakes applicable to healthy individuals in the United States and Canada. This new book is part of a series of books presenting dietary reference values for the intakes of nutrients. It establishes recommendations for energy, carbohydrate, fiber, fat, fatty acids, cholesterol, protein, and amino acids. This book presents new approaches and findings which include the following: The establishment of Estimated Energy Requirements at four levels of energy expenditure Recommendations for levels of physical activity to decrease risk of chronic disease The establishment of RDAs for dietary ca

publications.title: Groundwater Recharge and Flow: Approaches and Challenges for Monitoring and Modeling Using Remotely Sensed Data: Proceedings of a Workshop
publications.description: Water of appropriate quantity and quality is essential for drinking, sanitation, and food, energy, and industrial production for any society and is derived for most needs from surface- or groundwater sources. Studies suggest that groundwater use in irrigation globally is increasing in total volume as well as a percentage of all water used for irrigation, with the demand for groundwater resources increasing as available primary surface water supplies are depleted. Particularly in arid regions, groundwater may be the most accessible water supply for any purpose, leaving groundwater withdrawals concentrated in areas that are already experiencing water stress. Even in the presence of direct ground observations and measurements of the water table, quantitative evaluation of groundwater storage, flow, or recharge at different scales requires remotely sensed data and observations applied to groundwater models. publications.title: Prospects for Managed Underground Storage of Recoverable Water
publications.description: Growing demands for water in many parts of the nation are fueling the search for new approaches to sustainable water management, including how best to store water. Society has historically relied on dams and reservoirs, but problems such as high evaporation rates and a lack of suitable land for dam construction are driving interest in the prospect of storing water underground. Managed underground storage should be considered a valuable tool in a water manager's portfolio, although it poses its own unique challenges that need to be addressed through research and regulatory measures.

publications.title: The Next Decade of Discovery in Solar and Space Physics: Exploring and Safeguarding Humanity's Home in Space
publications.description: Groundbreaking advances in solar and space physics have provided key insights into the dynamic physical processes on the Sun and its influence on Earth, the near-Earth space environment, other planets in our solar system, and beyond. As we look to the next decade, future discoveries in the field will expand our knowledge of the cosmos and better prepare us for the impact of space weather events on critical systems and humanity. The Next Decade of Discovery in Solar and Space Physics: Exploring and Safeguarding Humanity's Home in Space, a National Academies' decadal survey, presents a prioritized strategy for basic and applied research to advance scientific understanding of the heliosphere and the origins of space weather, the Sun's interactions with other bodies in the solar system, and the interplanetary and interstellar mediums.
